q: Energy harnessing and utilization cause no significant environmental damage apart from resource depletion.
Air pollution, climate change, and water pollution are directly related environmental problems of energy production and consumption.
The environmental impacts of non-renewable and renewable sources of energy are generally the same.
Global warming is the gradual decrease in the average temperature of the Earth's surface.
Greenhouse gases such as $\text{CO}_2$, $\text{CH}_4$, and $\text{NOx}$ contribute to global warming.
Chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs) destroy the Earth's protective ozone layer, shielding it from harmful UV rays.
Acid rains are mainly caused by the release of sulfur dioxide ($\text{SO}_2$) and oxides of nitrogen ($\text{NOx}$) from burning fossil fuels.
Carbonic acid generated by carbon dioxide alone is a strong acid that significantly causes true acid rain.
Acidified clouds must release acid rain immediately above their source and cannot travel great distances.
Acid rains contribute to soil degradation, water pollution, and corrosion of the built environment.
The oil industry adds lead ($\text{Pb}$) to petrol to increase engine efficiency, without health concerns.
Leaded fumes from vehicles are known to cause mental development impairments in children.
Leakage of fuel oil from storage vessels, oil tankers, and pipelines is completely avoidable with modern technology.
Oil spills can damage land, vegetation, water bodies, and ecosystems.
Gas leaks and explosions are never associated with the harnessing of fossil fuels.
Strip-mined areas for coal, if not filled and revegetated, can cause water pollution from sediments and sulfur-bearing compounds.
Emissions from vehicles and power plants, such as unburned hydrocarbons and carbon monoxide, do not affect air quality.
Photovoltaics is considered an inherently unsafe technology compared to some other electricity generating technologies.
A photovoltaic module produces much less electricity over its life than was used in its production.
Photovoltaic systems generate considerable noise during operation.
PV cell technologies have higher environmental risks compared to other types of electric sources.
Chemicals used in PV cell manufacturing, installation, or disposal facilities can potentially be released into the environment.
Wind farms installed on agricultural land have one of the highest environmental impacts among energy sources.
Wind farms occupy more land area per kilowatt hour (kWh) of electricity generated than any other energy conversion system.
Modern wind turbine designs have increased the noise from turbines.
Wind turbines may create shadow flicker on nearby residences when the sun passes behind the turbine.
Hydroelectric power construction and operation have no air quality impacts.
Hydropower dams can significantly affect natural river systems and fish and wildlife populations.
Large dams in flat basins cause flooding of land and destruction of habitats.
Dams generally promote the unrestricted migration of river animals.
Dams serve as a heat sink, meaning the water in them is cooler than normal river water.
Geothermal power is generally considered a harmful source of energy.
Geothermal development typically has negative impacts that are entirely unavoidable.
Any large-scale construction and drilling operation for geothermal power can affect local economies.
Biomass power plants avoid concerns about air emissions and water use entirely, unlike fossil fuel plants.
Biomass power plants do not require water for cooling.
Land use impacts from biomass power production are primarily driven by the type of feedstock used.
Global warming emissions are not associated with growing and harvesting biomass feedstock.
Global warming emissions from the sourcing of biomass feedstock vary widely.
Hydroelectricity involves a conversion from gravitational potential energy to kinetic energy to electrical energy.
Tidal energy directly converts thermal energy to electrical energy.
Fossil fuels convert chemical energy to kinetic energy, then to electrical energy without a thermal stage.
Solar energy converts light and thermal energy directly into mechanical energy.
Nuclear energy involves a direct conversion from nuclear energy to electrical energy, bypassing thermal and kinetic stages.
Wind energy's conversion path is kinetic energy to thermal energy to electrical energy.
Bio-fuel energy conversion involves chemical energy to thermal energy to mechanical energy to electrical energy.
Geothermal energy converts thermal energy to kinetic energy to electrical energy.
One of the problems posed by large dams is that they are breeding grounds for mosquitoes and cause the spread of disease.
Dams restrict sediments that are crucial for fertile lands downstream.
The release of warm water from dams into rivers downstream does not affect animal life.
